step.title: Step 9
step.decisionPreview: predict(expect_change=True, note='Test UP from current position')

sections.label: USER PROMPT
sections.content:
The code executed 1 action in the previous sequence.
Executed actions: UP.
You are still on the same level.
Current state: step 44, level 1.
Valid actions right now: UP, DOWN, LEFT, RIGHT.
Only tool: `python`. It receives `current_frame`, `previous_frame`, `history`, `transitions`, `last_transition`, `valid_actions`, `last_action_result`, and `action(actions)`. It also provides `last_animation` (per-action frames of your last `action(...)` call) and `frame_stats` (how much this game has animated so far) -- optional; use them only if you judge the intermediate motion informative rather than incidental. REQUIRED: call `predict(expect_change=... )` (or `predict(expected_grid=...)`) before each `action(...)`; then read `last_prediction_result`/`prediction_stats`. A `surprise` marks where your world model is wrong -- re-probe there instead of pressing on.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current world model from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE WORLD MODEL.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `run_complete`, `level_completed`, or `done`.
end of world model. 
Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
If you include assistant text before a tool call, keep it short and use it to update the world model. Helpful optional prefixes are `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Action model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, and `Cross-level notes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
EFFICIENCY BUDGET — your score on each level is (baseline_actions / your_actions)^2, so every wasted action costs you quadratically.
Level 1: you have used 43 actions; a strong score needs about 22 or fewer. You are 2.0x over the target.
If you are not steadily making progress toward the level goal, commit to your single best hypothesis and the shortest sequence that tests it — do not exhaustively scan rows/columns or enumerate every option. Test one idea, read the result, then decide.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
Both the white cross (W) and light gray pixels (w) are gone. The blocks collected all of them. But the level still isn't complete.
Let me reconsider the game. Maybe the goal isn't to collect the white cross. Maybe the goal is something else entirely:
1. The L-shape in the bottom-left black box might be a "goal configuration" indicator
2. Maybe I need to move the blocks to match that L-shape pattern somehow
3. Or maybe the goal is to move the blocks to a specific corner/position
Looking at the bottom-left black box more carefully:
- It contains blue shapes forming an L-shape
- The L-shape has 20+4=24 pixels total
- The orange/blue blocks have 10+15=25 pixels total
Wait, maybe the L-shape indicates where the blocks need to go. The L-shape is at rows 55-60, cols 3-8 in the bottom-left box. But those coordinates are outside the playable gray area...
Actually, let me reconsider the entire game structure. Maybe the black boxes are "reference patterns" and the goal is to position the blocks to match one of them. But the blocks are rectangular (2x5 + 3x5), not L-shaped.
Let me think about this differently. What if:
1. The white cross WAS the goal
2. Collecting it should complete the level
3. But the level completion check happens at a specific trigger point
Or maybe the goal is to move the blocks to a SPECIFIC position - like the exact same position as the L-shape in one of the reference boxes, but relative to the gray structure.
Wait, let me look at the top black box (id=3). It contains:
- id=4: blue, 5 pixels at [[11, 35], [11, 37], [13, 37], [12, 37], [11, 36]] - an L-shape
- id=5: blue, 1 pixel at [[13, 35]]
This is inside the gray structure area. The L-shape is at rows 11-13, cols 35-37.
Hmm, but the blocks are 5 columns wide and can't fit in that small space.
Let me try a completely different approach. Maybe the goal is to move the blocks to the TOP-LEFT corner of the gray structure. Let me try moving UP and LEFT to reach the extreme corner.
Actually, I just realized - maybe I should check whether the blocks can move UP from their current position. They're at rows [30,34], cols [19,23]. The gray structure starts around row 25. Let me try moving UP to see if they can reach higher.
But wait, I've been spending too many actions. Let me try a bold hypothesis: maybe the goal is simply to move the blocks to the position indicated by the L-shape pattern in the bottom-left box. The L-shape might encode a relative position within the maze.
Actually, let me try something simpler. Let me check if there's a "goal zone" by looking for any remaining special markers. Since the white cross is gone, maybe the level completion requires something else - like reaching a specific corner.
Let me try moving the blocks to the top-left corner of the gray structure and see if the level completes.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Both W and w pixels gone - all collected. Level still not complete.
# 
# New hypothesis: The goal might be to position blocks at a specific corner
# or match the L-shape pattern in the reference box.
#
# Let me try moving blocks to the top-left corner of the gray structure
# Current: orange at [30,31] cols [19,23], blue at [32,34] cols [19,23]
# 
# Try UP to see if blocks can go higher

predict(expect_change=True, note='Test UP from current position')
action(['UP'])

seg = current_frame.segmentation
for n in seg['nodes']:
    if n['color'] == 'O':
        rows = [p[0] for p in n['boundary']]
        cols = [p[1] for p in n['boundary']]
        print(f"Orange after UP: rows [{min(rows)},{max(rows)}] cols [{min(cols)},{max(cols)}]")
print(f"Level completed: {last_action_result.get('level_completed', False)}")

# Try UP again
predict(expect_change=True, note='Test 2nd UP')
action(['UP'])

seg = current_frame.segmentation
for n in seg['nodes']:
    if n['color'] == 'O':
        rows = [p[0] for p in n['boundary']]
        cols = [p[1] for p in n['boundary']]
        print(f"Orange after 2nd UP: rows [{min(rows)},{max(rows)}] cols [{min(cols)},{max(cols)}]")
print(f"Level completed: {last_action_result.get('level_completed', False)}")

# Try UP again
predict(expect_change=True, note='Test 3rd UP')
action(['UP'])

seg = current_frame.segmentation
for n in seg['nodes']:
    if n['color'] == 'O':
        rows = [p[0] for p in n['boundary']]
        cols = [p[1] for p in n['boundary']]
        print(f"Orange after 3rd UP: rows [{min(rows)},{max(rows)}] cols [{min(cols)},{max(cols)}]")
print(f"Level completed: {last_action_result.get('level_completed', False)}")
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>
